title: Is PyTorch a hard requirement, or just the assumed default?
body:
Searching the repo, PyTorch is clearly assumed but I couldn't find explicit clarification on whether it's a hard requirement. This is related to #17 but specific enough for its own issue.

This matters because a stated goal is 'fostering architectural innovation', and torch's compile/runtime stack is biased toward Llama-style transformers[^1]. Over the 2.x versions, support and performance for ops outside that mold (_complex-valued ops, non-standard scan patterns, so on_) remains minimal or absent. Constraining to PyTorch + fixed GPU time risks producing results that are artifacts of these biases/constraints rather than genuine architectural insights-likely converging on weird Llama ablations that happen to fit the size constraint[^2]

Concrete example from my own work with [MEGALODON](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.08801) (trainable/interpretable reimplementation) to explore inductive bias:

- **PyTorch** ([repo](https://github.com/pszemraj/megalodon-enwik8/blob/6d49c580bfed1eb13203bf361991b8e08ab3e416/RESULTS.md)): 5.9% lower val loss than Llama with 10% fewer params on equal steps, but ~2.6x slower / ~1.7x more VRAM because **torch support for its complex ops is bad**.
- **JAX** ([repo](https://github.com/pszemraj/megalodon-enwik8-jax/blob/9b2c2c82c4dd2aaf798f791ce10618d04edc3a47/RESULTS.md)): native JIT compilation of those same ops closes the gap entirely:

| Model | Params | Val Loss @1200 | BPC | Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Megalodon** | 11.28M | 1.49 | 2.15 | ~3.2m |
| Llama | 12.49M | 1.53 | 2.21 | ~3.1m |

Relevance to this challenge: an arch that's demonstrably better per-parameter would never place on the leaderboard because the wallclock penalty is a PyTorch penalty, not a model penalty.

I don't have a clean fix-some ideas would be to include select alternative frameworks, normalizing for FLOP utilization instead of raw wallclock, or a separate "parameter-efficiency" track from the "train-in-10-min" track. Open to discussion. The broader point is that ML research is more constrained by framework assumptions than we tend to acknowledge, and this challenge-explicitly about architectural creativity-is a good place to surface this


[^1]: given that they made llama, I can't really blame them here
[^2]: check out the [bittensor pretrain subnet](https://github.com/macrocosm-os/pretraining) challenge + best models (aka subnet9/sn9, [some examples](https://huggingface.co/models?search=sn9)) and how things were optimized over time, also had a fixed size but unlimited training time, validation was on fineweb/similar. tl;dr a bunch of weird llama architecture ablations (at the time)

comments.body: You're welcome to use Jax, but please ensure your record is independently reproducible from purely the code in your /records folder.
